and entirely memorable (难忘的).
For example, it was such an amazing page-turner (引人入胜的书)
that I managed to completely lose oneself in (沉迷于...) it.
To be honest, reading it on an loudly overcrowded (拥挤的) train helped me significantly.

questions.question_text:
Describe a movie or TV show that taught you something important. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a Chinese TV show called "Nothing But Thirty."
To begin with, it was about three women trying to balance work, family, and personal dreams.
Honestly, I watched it about two years ago with my mum.
The story was so captivating (迷人的 / 吸引人的) that I couldn't stop watching every night.
Actually, the most memorable (难忘的) lesson I learned was that women should never lose themselves for others.
The show was deeply thought-provoking (发人深省的),
and it made me think about my own future.
Furthermore, it felt like reading a page-turner (引人入胜的书)
because every episode ended with a big surprise.
I think it was very educational (有教育意义的)
because it showed real life problems that many women face,
which means everyone can learn something important from it.

questions.question_text:
Describe a story someone told you that you still remember. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a story
my grandfather told me when I was about ten years old.
To begin with, it was a classic (经典的) folk tale about a poor but kind young man.
Honestly, the story wasn't trendy (时髦的) or modern at all,
but it was full of warm lessons.
My grandfather told me this story at a very overcrowded (拥挤的 (book fair)) book fair,
where we were waiting in a long line.
I remember I started to lose oneself in (沉迷于...) his words completely,
forgetting everything around me.
The main lesson was simple —
never judge a book by its cover (以貌取人),
because a person's true value comes from their heart,
not their appearance,
which is something I still think about today.
Actually, that story changed how I see people.

questions.question_text: What should the government do to make libraries better?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, the government should heavily fund public libraries,
because improving local Accessibility (可访问性 / 便捷性) boosts national education.
This is largely due to the fact that,
providing free resources significantly increases basic Literacy (读写能力 / 素养) rates among poor families.
For example, when a dedicated Bookworm (书呆子 / 爱书的人) has free digital tablets,
they can easily study anytime.

questions.question_text: Which one is better, paper books or e-books?

chunks.text: In my view, e-books are much better,
as they offer incredible convenience for modern daily travel.
The primary reason is that, carrying heavy physical books is exhausting,
whereas a digital device holds thousands of novels.
However, some traditional readers strongly prefer paper books
because they actively resist modern digital Consumerism (消费主义) and screen glare.

questions.question_text: Why should children listen to bedtime stories?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, listening to bedtime stories is vital,
because it profoundly stimulates a young child's Imagination (想象力).
This is crucial since, hearing complex narratives safely
helps kids logically process their own daily emotional struggles.
For instance, parents who quietly Hit the books (用功读书) with their toddlers
create a highly relaxing routine before sleep.

questions.question_text: Do Chinese children prefer ancient stories or fairy tales?

chunks.text: Admittedly, younger Chinese children strongly prefer magical fairy tales,
whereas older students slowly appreciate ancient historical myths.
The core issue is that, little kids are naturally drawn to extremely colorful,
highly Impulsive (冲动的) magical heroes.
Conversely (相反地), understanding ancient dynasties actually requires them
to successfully Read between the lines (读懂言外之意) to learn deep moral lessons.

questions.question_text: Has the internet reduced the attention span required to read long novels?

chunks.text: Undeniably, the modern internet has severely ruined our ability to focus,
because short videos deliver instant gratification (满足感).
As a result, a teenager's natural Attention span (注意力持续时间)
is completely shattered by constantly scrolling through endless social media feeds.
Consequently, reading a huge thick novel now feels incredibly tedious (乏味的) and boring to many young internet users.

questions.question_text: Is storytelling an essential part of preserving a culture's history?

chunks.text: It is widely acknowledged that, vocal storytelling is absolutely essential,
as it successfully guarantees the Preservation (保存) of local heritage.
This means that, before written languages existed,
tribal elders transferred vital survival wisdom through spoken poems.
Therefore, verbal traditions beautifully keep ancient societal customs highly relevant and alive for the next generation.

questions.question_text:
Describe an occasion when many people laughed together. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe a highly memorable (难忘的) school trip,
because it was truly a joyful (快乐的) experience.
Actually, we were on a bus when our teacher suddenly decided to crack a joke (讲笑话).
Initially, there was an awkward (尴尬的) silence since nobody understood the punchline.
However, he then made a completely hilarious (极滑稽的) face that was surprisingly captivating (迷人的).
Consequently, the entire class burst into laughter (突然大笑) at the exact same time.
I think, the positive energy was incredibly contagious (有感染力的) among all the students.
Ultimately, it proved that laughter is the best medicine (笑是最好的良药),
making it a very amusing (有趣的) memory.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time you saw someone show their feelings very openly. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about my close friend, Lily.
It happened last year at a birthday party.
Actually, the host told a joke that was completely hilarious (极滑稽的).
Honestly, nobody expected it to be so funny.
Lily just burst into laughter (突然大笑) and could not stop for a long time.
To be honest, it was a little awkward (尴尬的) because everyone else had already stopped laughing.
For example, she was holding her stomach and had tears in her eyes.
I think her reaction was very amusing (有趣的) to watch.
Furthermore, her laughter was totally contagious (有感染力的 (laughter)),
which made the whole room start laughing again,
which turned the party into a really happy moment.
In the end, I felt very warm inside because it was a truly joyful experience.

questions.question_text:
Describe a comedy movie or live performance that made you laugh. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about a comedy movie called *Kung Fu Hustle*.
To begin with, it is a Chinese movie with Stephen Chow as the main actor.
Honestly, his acting is so captivating (迷人的) that I could not stop watching.
The story is about a man who wants to join a gang,
which made me laugh a lot.
For example, the funniest part is when Stephen Chow tries to crack a joke (讲笑话)
in a very serious situation, and everyone around him looks confused.
Actually, that moment was so funny because it was so unexpected.
In the end, watching this movie was a very joyful (快乐的) experience for me.
I really believe that laughter is the best medicine (笑是最好的良药).
To be honest, this movie is truly memorable (难忘的)
because it made me feel happy every time I think about it.

questions.question_text: Why do people laugh in their daily lives?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, people laugh daily because humor is a Universal (普遍的) human mechanism for safely coping with stress.
This is because, sharing a clever joke strongly promotes deep emotional Bonding (建立纽带) between hardworking colleagues and close friends.
For instance, individuals with a warm Sense of humor (幽默感) frequently use laughter to navigate awkward situations easily.

questions.question_text: Why do people smile when they take photos? Is it a genuine smile or a fake one?

chunks.text: In my view, smiling for photos is often a forced social expectation
rather than a completely genuine psychological reaction.
The primary reason is that, cultural norms demand us
to clearly look Expressive (表现力丰富的) and incredibly happy in captured memories.
As an illustration, many people will painfully Keep a straight face (板着脸 / 忍住不笑)
until the camera actually flashes, instantly faking sudden joy.

questions.question_text: Should we express our emotions immediately or hide them?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, balancing our emotions is crucial,
because constant emotional Suppression (压抑) leads to severe mental health issues.

However, one should not always broadly Wear one's heart on one's sleeve (真情流露) during highly formal business meetings.

questions.question_text: Regarding showing emotions, are there any differences between women and men?

Consequently, modern progressive education heavily focuses on significantly improving

questions.question_text: Is humor an essential tool for effective leadership in the workplace?

chunks.text: Undeniably, workplace humor is a vital leadership trait,
as it dramatically improves the overall Accessibility (可接近性) of a busy manager.
This means that, exhausted employees feel significantly more comfortable

Therefore, appropriate office banter effectively reduces tension
and steadily increases daily team productivity levels.

questions.question_text: How does cultural background influence what people find funny?

chunks.text: It is widely acknowledged that, cultural heritage entirely shapes comedy,
because most jokes heavily rely on shared historical context.
This is necessary since, a clever linguistic pun (双关语) frequently makes absolutely no logical sense
when literally translated abroad.
For instance, dry British sarcasm (讽刺) might tragically seem extremely rude
to someone from a more formal Eastern background.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between friendships at work and those outside of work?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, workplace relationships are often strictly professional,
whereas outside friendships offer genuine emotional Companionship (陪伴 / 友谊).
This is because, a standard Colleague (同事) might just be a shallow Fair-weather friend (酒肉朋友)
who disappears when office projects fail.
For instance, daily workplace Interaction (互动) is usually slightly Superficial (肤浅的)
compared to the deep bonds built with lifelong childhood peers.

questions.question_text: At what age do you think people start to have friendships?

chunks.text: In my view, people broadly form basic friendships during early kindergarten,
as toddlers naturally seek playmates to share toys.
The primary reason is that, constantly seeking social connection
is a completely Universal (普遍的) trait built into human biology.
As an illustration, these early connections strongly aid essential Psychological (心理的) development,
even if the kids rarely See eye to eye (看法一致 / 所见略同).

questions.question_text: Do you think music is beneficial for children at school?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, music is highly beneficial,
because it significantly enhances memory
and radically boosts creative problem-solving skills.
This is crucial since, safely learning instruments
requires intense discipline
and steadily builds strong teamwork during group performances.
Therefore, happily playing in a school band
completely fosters true Lifelong (终身的) friendships,
proving that A friend in need is a friend indeed (患难见真情)
when practicing tough songs.

questions.question_text: Do you think a child's relationship with friends can be replaced by that with parents?

chunks.text: Admittedly, strict parents provide essential physical safety,
but they absolutely cannot entirely replace the unique equality found in peer friendships.
The core issue is that, an inherent (固有的) hierarchical power dynamic heavily exists
between a demanding mother and a growing child.
Consequently, anxious kids desperately need close friends of their own exact age
to discuss private teenage secrets completely freely.

questions.question_text: Has social media changed the definition of "friendship" in modern society?

chunks.text: Undeniably, online social networks have cheaply altered friendship,
as naive people now wrongly equate mere digital followers with actual true friends.
As lonely individuals constantly scroll online,
they wildly prioritize collecting superficial internet likes
rather than securely building profound offline relationships.
For example, a depressed teenager might easily have thousands of virtual contacts
but still feel devastatingly isolated in real life.

questions.question_text: Is it possible to maintain a lifelong friendship despite living in different countries?

chunks.text: It is widely acknowledged that, vast long-distance friendships
can survive beautifully, provided both parties put in continuous mutual effort.
This practically means that, advanced communication technologies like video calling
remarkably make maintaining daily emotional contact incredibly convenient globally.
For instance, separated best friends will eagerly schedule weekly online chats
to consistently share their ongoing personal lifestyle updates.

questions.question_text: Why is it difficult for some people to relax?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, relaxing is incredibly difficult,
because modern Urban (城市的) life is intensely competitive and highly demanding mentally.
This is because, outrageously heavy workloads and severe financial pressure
leave adults in a constant state of deep anxiety.
For instance, helplessly struggling to safely secure basic Affordability (可负担性) for housing
strongly prevents workers from genuinely enjoying their brief weekend downtime.

questions.question_text: Which is more important, mental relaxation or physical relaxation?

chunks.text: In my view, deep mental relaxation is slightly more critical,
as terrible psychological burnout completely drains all bodily energy rapidly.
The primary reason is that, quietly resting on a soft sofa
is utterly useless if your mind is heavily panicked.
As an illustration, gently practicing mindful meditation securely provides much deeper emotional relief
than just vaguely Superficial (表面的) physical resting like sleeping all day.

questions.question_text: Why do people prefer to live in new houses?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, newly built properties are highly desirable,
because they require vastly less stressful structural maintenance overall.
This is largely due to the fact that,
modern apartment constructions brilliantly feature excellent smart technology
and highly efficient heating systems.
For example, a shiny brand new condo smoothly serves as
a brilliant long-term financial Investment (投资)
without hidden expensive plumbing repair costs.

questions.question_text: How has the "Work From Home" trend changed the way people design their houses?

chunks.text: Admittedly, remote working has totally revolutionized modern home design,
as completely dedicated quiet office spaces are now entirely essential.
Furthermore, tired citizens increasingly prioritize heavily having strong structural soundproofing
and exceptionally large windows to drastically improve their daily focus.
Consequently, a simple suburban bedroom must now simultaneously function beautifully
as a strict professional environment and a relaxing sanctuary.

questions.question_text: What are the social consequences of housing shortages in major cities?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, an extreme modern housing Shortage (短缺)
causes massive societal inequality,
because urban property prices inevitably completely Go through the roof (飞涨).
The core issue is that,
passionate young university graduates are completely priced out,
abruptly destroying their Lifelong (终身的) dreams of urban stability.
Therefore, failing to successfully help local youth quickly Get on the property ladder (置业 / 买第一套房)
leads to widespread anger and plummeting (骤降) birth rates.

questions.question_text: Should the government regulate housing prices to ensure everyone has a place to live?

chunks.text: It is widely acknowledged that,
strict state market Regulation (法规 / 管制) is absolutely required,
because securing a basic home is a fundamental human right.
This is necessary since,
completely uncontrolled greedy corporate landlords will absolutely exploit (剥削)
highly vulnerable citizens for maximum pure financial profit.
For instance, legally implementing fair rent control policies
beautifully ensures that low-income tired workers can always safely afford
A roof over one's head (栖身之处).

questions.question_text: Why do some grown-ups hate to throw out old things?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, older adults fiercely keep old items,

perfectly tied to specific personal historical belongings.

but truly One man's trash is another man's treasure (变废为宝) to lonely grandparents.

questions.question_text: What do you think influences people to buy new things?

chunks.text: In my view, clever social media advertising heavily influences modern society,
as it constantly loudly promotes a greedy Materialistic (物质主义的) lifestyle.
The primary reason is that, massive greedy corporations intentionally create
an artificial sense of daily urgency to massively boost retail sales.
As an illustration, toxic modern peer pressure strongly forces young teenagers
to foolishly buy the newest smart devices
simply to desperately look wealthy.

questions.question_text: Do young people appreciate antiques and old things as much as older people?

chunks.text: Undeniably, the modern younger generation strongly prefers shiny new gadgets,
whereas wise seniors truly deeply respect historical cultural artifacts.
This is largely due to the fact that,
busy young minds vastly prioritize immediate digital convenience
entirely over long-term physical historical preservation.
Consequently, only a highly rare few educated teenagers

as a genuinely wise financial Investment (投资).

questions.question_text: Why do people like to go to museums to see old objects?

The core issue is that, closely observing exactly how our strong ancestors creatively survived
physically grounds us heavily in a chaotic modern world.
Therefore, people happily eagerly pay the easy Affordability (购买力) of a remarkably cheap ticket

questions.question_text: Is the culture of consumerism (buying things for pleasure) sustainable for the planet?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, extreme Consumerism (消费主义) is utterly catastrophic (灾难性的),
as it rapidly fatally depletes Earth's highly finite global natural resources.
This is primarily since, the deeply blind pursuit of endless cheap luxury goods
completely destroys all realistic hopes for global environmental Sustainability (可持续性).
For example, aggressively producing billions of cheap plastic toys

For instance, purposely designing smartphones

questions.question_text: Who prefers long journeys, young people or old people?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, young people prefer long journeys,
whereas older individuals usually avoid them.
To explain, the youth have more energy for eco-tourism (生态旅游)
and care less about their carbon footprint (碳足迹) when traveling far.
For instance, many university students take month-long backpacking trips across different continents.

questions.question_text: What do people prepare before a journey?

chunks.text: Undoubtedly, travelers pack essential items,
and they also plan their itineraries carefully.
This is because, thorough preparation (准备) helps them avoid
the stressful hustle and bustle (熙熙攘攘) of navigating unfamiliar and crowded places.
For example, booking hotels and researching local transit routes in advance
ensures a much smoother trip.

questions.question_text: Why do people prefer traveling in their own countries rather than foreign countries?

chunks.text: In my view, domestic travel is much cheaper,
and it avoids language barriers.
Specifically, people do not want a long commute (通勤)
or to deal with massive airport congestion (拥堵) during short holidays.
As an illustration, driving to a nearby province
is significantly easier than flying internationally.

questions.question_text: Do you think air pollution comes mostly from mobile vehicles?

chunks.text: To a certain extent, cars are a major source,
but factories contribute heavily too.
Evidently, industrial exhaust and vehicular emission (排放)
both accumulate (积累) in the atmosphere to cause severe smog.
For instance, many manufacturing hubs have noticeably worse air quality
than busy urban centers.

questions.question_text: Do you think people need to change the way of transportation drastically to protect the environment?

chunks.text: Absolutely, a significant shift is required
if we want to save our planet.
Fundamentally, our current reliance on fossil fuels
completely undermines long-term environmental sustainability (可持续性).
Therefore, switching globally to electric trains or hydrogen-powered buses
will dramatically lower harmful pollution levels.

questions.question_text: Has the ability to travel cheaply (budget airlines) changed how people view the world?

chunks.text: Certainly, budget travel has broadened perspectives,
since more people can explore internationally.
Consequently, average citizens can escape the daily rush hour (高峰期)
to experience foreign cultures firsthand.
For example, affordable flights allow students to witness global diversity,
making them much more tolerant.

questions.question_text: How can we know if a child has talent or not?

chunks.text: Essentially, parents can spot talent early,
especially when children show innate (天生的) skills.
Specifically, a child might naturally excel at tasks
without needing intense preparation (准备).
For example, a brainy (聪明的) toddler who solves complex puzzles easily
demonstrates high intellectual potential (潜力).

questions.question_text: Is it a waste of time if people don't use their talents?

chunks.text: Honestly, it is a tragic loss,
because society misses out on their gifts.
Therefore, if we do not nurture (培养 / 后天教育) our abilities,
we fail to contribute meaningfully.
For instance, a musical genius who ignores their gift
limits their own creative emission (排放) into the world.

questions.question_text: Are children born smart, or do they become smart through learning?

chunks.text: In reality, it is a combination,
since both genetics and environment matter.
Certainly, natural intelligence (智力) exists,
but education refines it because practice makes perfect (熟能生巧).
As an example, rigorous academic (学术的) training
can make an average student highly successful over time.

questions.question_text: Is being intelligent always a good thing? Are there limitations?

chunks.text: Admittedly, high intellect is beneficial,
yet it can cause social isolation.
Frequently, brilliant individuals focus on one niche
instead of being a jack of all trades (万金油 / 博而不精),
causing them to struggle socially.
For example, a math prodigy (神童) might feel completely misunderstood
by regular classmates.

questions.question_text: Do you think the education system stifles creativity by focusing too much on academic scores?

chunks.text: Absolutely, strict testing restricts imagination,
and it limits independent thought.
Consequently, schools reward memorization (死记硬背)
rather than original problem-solving skills.
For instance, art and music programs are often canceled
to make more time for standardized test preparation.

questions.question_text: Can creativity be learned, or is it an innate quality?

chunks.text: Personally, I believe it can be developed,
although some have natural inclinations (倾向).
Fundamentally, being exposed to diverse experiences
teaches the brain to form new connections.
For example, taking painting classes can unlock
a person's hidden artistic abilities over time.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person you know who works in a family business. (Examiner Note: Focus your description on the business place itself) You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: First of all, I want to describe a diligent (勤奋的) baker I know,
who is highly entrepreneurial (有创业精神的), and who owns a great shop.
Essentially, his bakery is a traditional mom and pop store (夫妻店).
Currently, his aging father continues to run the show (掌管 / 主持大局).
However, they plan to pass the torch (薪火相传 / 交接) to him soon.
Honestly, they sell authentic (正宗的 / 真实的) sourdough bread that is universally loved.
Furthermore, their business is extremely prosperous (繁荣的) due to their hard work.
Overall, they are a very reputable (声誉好的) and dedicated (敬业的) family,
providing exceptional (杰出的) service.

questions.question_text:
Describe a restaurant or cafe that you enjoy going to. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a small noodle shop near my home in Chengdu.
Actually, it is a family business,
and the owner's mother and father run the show (掌管 / 主持大局) together every day.
To begin with, they serve really tasty, authentic (正宗的 / 真实的) Sichuan noodles with spicy broth.
Honestly, the family is very diligent (勤奋的), and they open the shop early every morning.
I go there about twice a week because the food is cheap and delicious.
For example, my favourite dish is dan dan noodles with extra chilli oil.
I think the owner has a very entrepreneurial (有创业精神的) spirit,
which I really admire.
In the end, the shop has become quite prosperous (繁荣的) because many local people love eating there.
It feels warm and friendly, like eating at a neighbour's home.

questions.question_text:
Describe a place in your city that has changed a lot over the years. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I want to talk about a small shopping street near my home.
To begin with, it used to be full of little family shops.
My favourite was a mom and pop store (夫妻店) that sold fresh bread and drinks.
The owners were very dedicated (敬业的) people who worked long hours every day.
Honestly, they were known for their exceptional (杰出的 (service)) service,
and everyone in the area loved them.
They were also quite reputable (声誉好的) in our community.
In the end, the old couple had to pass the torch (薪火相传 / 交接) to a big supermarket chain,
which moved in and changed everything.
I think this change is a little sad because the street lost its warm feeling,
which means our neighbourhood feels less friendly now.
Actually, I really miss those small shops.

questions.question_text: Would you like to start a family business?

chunks.text: Personally, I would avoid it,
because mixing family and work is stressful.
Specifically, managing a startup (初创公司) requires intense focus
that can easily damage personal relationships.
For example, arguments over financial decisions
might cause a family to go out of business (破产 / 倒闭).

questions.question_text: What are the advantages and disadvantages of family businesses?

chunks.text: Generally, they offer deep trust,
yet they often lack professional management.
Undoubtedly, competition (竞争) is fierce,
and relatives may not have the innate (固有的) skills to survive.
For instance, a small family shop might lose out
to a larger, more efficient corporation (大公司 / 企业).

questions.question_text: Why do young people start their own business?

chunks.text: Mainly, they desire independence,
and they want to pursue their passions.
Furthermore, the modern economy (经济) offers immense potential (潜力) for innovative ideas to succeed quickly.
As an illustration, many tech graduates create software apps to become their own bosses.

questions.question_text: How do large international corporations impact local family-owned businesses?

chunks.text: Sadly, they often destroy local shops,
as they offer much lower prices.
Essentially, it is a cut-throat (残酷的) environment
where big fish eat little fish (大鱼吃小鱼 / 弱肉强食).
For example, a giant supermarket opening nearby
usually bankrupts small neighborhood grocery stores.

questions.question_text: Is it better for a city's economy to have many small shops or a few large shopping malls?

chunks.text: In my opinion, diverse small shops are better,
because they distribute wealth evenly.
Conversely, a monopoly (垄断) by massive malls removes character from the city streets.
Therefore, supporting local vendors keeps the community vibrant and financially stable.

questions.question_text: How has the rise of online shopping changed the physical landscape of city centers?

chunks.text: Noticeably, many physical stores have closed down,
and streets look emptier.
As a result, high retail rents force traditional brands
to move entirely to e-commerce platforms.
For instance, former shopping districts are now being converted
into residential apartments or cafes.

questions.question_text:
Describe a toy that you loved in your childhood. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I will describe my wooden train set,
which was highly educational (有教育意义的), and which kept me busy.
Honestly, looking at it now takes me on a trip down memory lane (追忆往昔).
Furthermore, it made me feel very imaginative (富于想象的) as a kid.
Actually, the wood was incredibly durable (耐用的), so it never broke.
Consequently, it became my absolute pride and joy (掌上明珠 / 最得意的珍宝).
Moreover, it reminds me of my prosperous (富裕的) and happy childhood.
Overall, this authentic (真实的) memory is very precious (珍贵的) to me.
Finally, playing with it was simple child's play (小儿科 / 极容易的事),
making me feel deeply nostalgic (怀旧的) today.

questions.question_text:
Describe a gift you received as a child that you still remember. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, the gift I still remember is a set of building blocks
my father gave me on my sixth birthday.
Actually, the blocks were very durable (耐用的) and never broke,
even after years of heavy use.
I think my father chose them because he wanted me to be more imaginative (富于想象的) and creative.
Honestly, I built so many things with those blocks —
houses, towers, and even little towns.
Of course, it sounds like child's play (小儿科 / 极容易的事),
but for me it was a serious hobby.
To be honest, every time I see old toys like these,
I feel very nostalgic (怀旧的).
In the end, those blocks are still precious (珍贵的) to me
because they remind me of happy times with my father,
which always makes me feel warm inside.

questions.question_text:
Describe something old that your family has kept for a long time. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about an old toy car
that my family has kept for over twenty years.
To begin with, it was given to me when I was about four years old.
Honestly, it is a small red car made of metal,
and my parents say it was quite expensive at the time.
My family keeps it because it brings back authentic (真实的 (memories)) memories of my early childhood,
which makes everyone feel warm and happy.
Actually, my mum calls it her pride and joy (掌上明珠 / 最得意的珍宝)
because it reminds her of my younger days.
Furthermore, she says it was part of a prosperous (富裕的 (childhood)) time for our family.
I think it was also educational (有教育意义的)
because it helped me learn about colours and shapes.
In the end, looking at it is always a lovely trip down memory lane (追忆往昔).

questions.question_text: Do boys and girls prefer different kinds of toys?

chunks.text: Traditionally, yes, but modern parents are encouraging more gender-neutral options.
Historically, society pushed dolls for girls and action figures for boys
to shape their early socialization (社会化).
Today, educational building blocks are equally popular for both genders' development (发展).

questions.question_text: How do toys that were popular in the past compare with toys that are popular now?

chunks.text: Undeniably, older toys were simpler,
while modern toys are highly digital.
Consequently, classic wooden toys required more physical interaction (互动)
compared to today's screen-based games.
For example, playing board games demands more face-to-face communication
than solitary mobile applications.

questions.question_text: What should people consider when choosing a toy for a child?

chunks.text: Primarily, safety is crucial, but educational value is also highly important.
Specifically, a good toy should stimulate cognitive (认知的) growth and spark creativity (创造力).
For instance, puzzles are excellent because they teach children
how to play by the rules (遵守规则) safely.

questions.question_text: Should kids be permitted to pick their own toys at a toy store?

chunks.text: Occasionally, it is fine, provided parents set a strict budget beforehand.
Otherwise, children might become a spoiled brat (被宠坏的孩子)
if they always get whatever they demand.
As an illustration, allowing them to choose a small reward
teaches them about the economy (经济 / 金钱观念).

questions.question_text: Do educational toys really help children become smarter?

chunks.text: Absolutely, they provide a strong foundation,
though they cannot replace parenting.
In fact, toys that challenge the brain
can give kids an edge in future competition (竞争).
For example, learning robotics early on
develops critical thinking and spatial awareness.

questions.question_text: Is it harmful for children to own too many toys?

chunks.text: Definitely, an excess of toys causes distraction,
and it ruins appreciation.
Ultimately, if parents wrap someone in cotton wool (溺爱 / 过分保护) by buying everything,
children lose focus.
Therefore, having fewer items forces kids
to be more resourceful and imaginative during playtime.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when the electricity suddenly went off where you live. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe an unexpected (意想不到的) power cut,
which was highly inconvenient (不便的),
and which left me completely in the dark (蒙在鼓里 / 在黑暗中).
Honestly, I was trying to finish an urgent (紧急的) report for my boss.
Suddenly, my computer shut down,
making me feel totally frustrated (沮丧的 / 挫败的).
Furthermore, the elevator in my building was out of order (故障).
Consequently, I felt entirely helpless (无助的) and trapped inside my apartment.
Luckily, my laptop had a very durable (耐用的) battery.
Ultimately, it managed to save the day (挽救局面) so I could email the document.
Overall, it taught me not to waste precious (宝贵的) time.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when your phone or computer stopped working. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: Actually, this happened about two years ago
when my laptop suddenly stopped working right before an important exam.
To be honest, it was very unexpected (意想不到的)
because the laptop was not very old.
To begin with, the screen just went black,
and I had no idea what was wrong.
I felt completely helpless (无助的) because all my study notes were inside.
Honestly, I tried restarting it many times, but nothing worked.
Furthermore, I took it to a repair shop,
where the worker told me the hard drive was broken,
which meant I lost almost all my files.
It was so inconvenient (不便的) during that busy time.
I felt really frustrated (沮丧的) because I had worked so hard on those notes.
In the end, I was completely in the dark (蒙在鼓里 / 在黑暗中) about how to prepare,
and I learned to always back up my files.

questions.question_text:
Describe an event that did not go as planned. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about a class presentation
that did not go as planned.
To begin with, I had to show a slideshow in front of my classmates.
Honestly, when I walked to the front,
the projector was completely out of order (故障).
It was a very urgent (紧急的) situation because my teacher was waiting.
I wasted a lot of precious (宝贵的) time trying to fix it.
Fortunately, my friend had a small portable speaker and a laptop,
and she really helped to save the day (挽救局面).
I used her laptop instead,
and the presentation went well after that.
Actually, I learned that technology is not always durable (耐用的),
so you should always have a backup plan.
For example, now I always bring extra files on a USB.
In the end, this experience made me a more careful and prepared person.

questions.question_text: When will equipment break normally? Why?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, machines break after years of use,
since they naturally degrade over time.
Basically, constant daily friction causes unavoidable wear and tear (磨损) on the internal mechanical parts.
For example, a car engine will inevitably fail completely
if the owner never changes the oil.

questions.question_text: Do people buy a new one when something is broken, or repair it?

chunks.text: Nowadays, most people simply buy replacements,
because modern gadgets are notoriously difficult to fix.
Sadly, planned obsolescence (废弃 / 淘汰) has created a toxic throwaway culture (用完即弃的文化) globally.
Consequently, throwing away a cracked smartphone and upgrading
is often cheaper than paying for a professional repair.

questions.question_text: Which do you think is better for production: using machines or making things by hand?

chunks.text: In my opinion, machines are far superior,
as they ensure speed and absolute precision.
Undoubtedly, automated manufacturing (制造业) guarantees a much higher level
of product reliability (可靠性).
For instance, modern robots in car factories
can assemble complex parts flawlessly without ever getting tired.

questions.question_text: Does the place where you live often experience power cuts?

chunks.text: Fortunately, blackouts are incredibly rare here,
because the local power grid is highly advanced.
Actually, due to rapid urban development (发展),
the government strictly maintains the electrical infrastructure.
Therefore, they follow the rule of don't fix what isn't broken (没坏就别修),
avoiding unnecessary disruptions.

questions.question_text: Are modern societies too dependent on electricity and technology?

chunks.text: Absolutely, we are entirely reliant on power,
and our lives halt without it.
Essentially, this massive dependency (依赖) means that a simple power outage
can shut down hospitals and banks.
As a result, people cannot even buy food
if the electronic payment systems crash.

questions.question_text: Is the "throwaway culture" (buying new instead of repairing) sustainable for the environment?

chunks.text: Definitely not, it is highly destructive,
since it generates massive amounts of electronic waste.
Fundamentally, constant garbage disposal actively ruins
global ecological sustainability (可持续性).
Instead, we must use our creativity (创造力)
to recycle old materials and protect our fragile planet.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you encouraged someone to do something that he/she didn't want to do. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about my best friend,
who felt completely frustrated (沮丧的),
and who was incredibly reluctant (不情愿的) to join a public speaking competition.
Honestly, I knew this was an urgent (紧急的) opportunity for her growth.
Therefore, I tried to be highly supportive (支持的) and persuasive (有说服力的).
Basically, I managed to talk her into (说服) joining the contest.
Furthermore, I reminded her that two heads are better than one (三个臭皮匠顶个诸葛亮 / 集思广益),
offering to help her practice.
Eventually, she decided to give it a shot (试一试).
Ultimately, she realized it was a wise (明智的) decision.
Overall, she was deeply appreciative (感激的) after winning third place.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you helped a friend through a difficult situation. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about my close friend, Li Wei,
who had a really hard time last year
when he failed an important exam.
Honestly, he was very reluctant (不情愿的) to try again
because he felt so low.
To begin with, I listened to him carefully
and tried to be very supportive (支持的) during those sad days.
Actually, I managed to talk him into something (说服某人做某事) positive —
I persuaded him to join a study group with me.
I used very persuasive (有说服力的) words
because I knew he had the ability to pass,
which meant he finally believed in himself again.
For example, I reminded him of his past successes every day.
In the end, he passed the next exam.
Afterwards, he was truly appreciative (感激的) and said I changed his life.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person who changed your mind about something. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about my university professor, Mr. Chen.
To begin with, he changed my mind about working in a group for projects.
Honestly, I always preferred to work alone
because I thought other people would slow me down.
At that time, I felt really frustrated (沮丧的) when group work was required.
However, Mr. Chen gave me some very wise (明智的) advice.
He said that finishing a big project alone felt urgent (紧急的) and stressful,
which meant I was missing good ideas from others.
He always said that two heads are better than one (三个臭皮匠顶个诸葛亮),
so he told me to just give it a shot (试一试).
Actually, I tried it and the result was much better.
In the end, I felt happy and more open-minded about working with other people.

questions.question_text: How can leaders encourage their employees?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, good managers provide clear goals,
and they reward hard work.
Specifically, effective leadership (领导力) requires bosses to actively lead by example (以身作则)
rather than just shouting orders.
For instance, a manager who stays late to help the team
will easily inspire loyalty and dedication.

questions.question_text: When should parents encourage their children?

chunks.text: In my opinion, parents should offer praise when kids try new things.
Fundamentally, facing failure is scary,
so children need constant emotional motivation (动力) to build their confidence.
Therefore, cheering for a child after they lose a sports match
helps them bounce back quickly.

questions.question_text: Why do some people like to ask others for advice?

chunks.text: Honestly, many individuals lack self-confidence,
so they seek external validation.
Furthermore, asking for professional guidance (指导) ensures a much higher level of reliability (可靠性)
when making choices.
For example, consulting a financial advisor prevents people
from making terrible investments with their savings.

questions.question_text: What important decisions should be made by teenagers themselves?

chunks.text: Ideally, youths should choose their own hobbies,
and they must select their own friends.
Consequently, making these social choices helps them reduce parental dependency (依赖)
and learn to handle peer pressure (同伴压力).
As a result, a teenager who picks their own sports club
becomes much more independent.

questions.question_text: Is positive reinforcement (encouragement) more effective than criticism?

chunks.text: Absolutely, praise builds trust,
whereas harsh criticism (批评) often causes deep resentment.
Actually, people are much more likely to improve their behavior
when they receive gentle, constructive criticism (建设性批评) instead of anger.
For instance, praising a student's effort before correcting their math errors
yields much better grades.

questions.question_text: How does peer pressure differ from positive encouragement?

chunks.text: Fundamentally, pressure forces conformity,
while encouragement sparks genuine personal growth.
Essentially, true mentorship (导师制度) lifts a person up,
whereas toxic peer influence drags them down into bad habits.
To illustrate, forcing a friend to skip class is pressure,
but studying together is pure encouragement.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time you received good service from a staff member in a shop/company. (Examiner Note: Focus your description on the place where this happened) You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about a recent shopping trip,
which was incredibly efficient (高效的),
and where I received impeccable (无懈可击的 / 完美的) service.
Honestly, the shop assistant was extremely courteous (礼貌的) and attentive (体贴的 / 殷勤的) to my needs.
Actually, she decided to go above and beyond (尽心尽力) to find my exact shoe size.
Furthermore, she was quite persuasive (有说服力的) without being overly pushy.
Throughout the process, she helped me with a smile (面带微笑).
Consequently, I felt absolutely satisfied (满意的) with my purchase.
Truly, they managed to treat someone like royalty (奉若上宾) in that store.
Finally, I left feeling deeply appreciative (感激的) for her wonderful help.

questions.question_text:
Describe a restaurant or cafe where you had a great experience. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I want to talk about a small café called "The Green Cup"
in the city centre of Chengdu.
To begin with, it is a cosy place with warm lights and soft music.
I went there last summer with my best friend.
Honestly, I ordered a matcha latte and a slice of chocolate cake,
and both were delicious.
The staff were very attentive (体贴的 / 殷勤的)
and always checked if we needed anything.
Actually, the service was impeccable (无懈可击的 / 完美的),
which made us feel very welcome.
The waiters were also courteous (礼貌的) and smiled the whole time.
I think the staff really go above and beyond (超越期望 / 尽心尽力)
to make every customer happy,
which is something I do not always see in other cafés.
In the end, I left feeling completely satisfied (满意的)
because the food, drinks, and service were all wonderful.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when a salesperson helped you choose a product. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about a time I went shopping
for a new laptop at a big electronics store in my city.
To begin with, I had no idea which model to buy
because there were so many choices.
Honestly, the salesperson was very efficient (高效的)
and asked me a few quick questions about my needs.
For example, he explained each laptop clearly
and was quite persuasive (有说服力的 (sales)),
which helped me feel sure about my final choice.
Furthermore, he treated me like a very important customer
and seemed to treat someone like royalty (奉若上宾)
throughout the whole visit.
He always spoke with a smile (面带微笑), so I felt very comfortable.
In the end, I bought a laptop that was perfect for my studies,
and I think I was really appreciative (感激的) of his kind help.

questions.question_text: Do you think expensive services are necessarily good services?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, high prices do not guarantee quality,
although they often raise expectations.
In reality, some luxury brands focus entirely on marketing
rather than the actual hospitality (服务业 / 好客) provided.
For example, a cheap local diner might offer much better service
and give you more bang for your buck (物超所值) than a fancy restaurant.

questions.question_text: Which one is better, face-to-face service or online service?

chunks.text: In my opinion, physical service is superior,
because it provides a crucial human touch (人情味).
Undoubtedly, talking to a real person provides clear guidance (指导)
when solving complex issues.
Therefore, if a financial transaction (交易) fails,
a human bank teller can reassure the customer immediately.

questions.question_text: Why do people buy things that they clearly don't need?

chunks.text: Fundamentally, impulsive consumption (消费) happens frequently,
because advertising creates strong artificial desires.
Moreover, a buyer's primary motivation (动机) is often emotional satisfaction
rather than absolute practical necessity.
For instance, many young shoppers purchase unnecessary trendy clothing
just to feel a temporary sense of happiness.

questions.question_text: Do you often go over your budget when you go shopping?

chunks.text: Personally, I rarely overspend,
since I practice strict budgeting (预算) every month.
Actually, I believe the old saying
that the customer is always right (顾客就是上帝)
is a marketing trap designed to make us buy more.
As a result, I always make a shopping list
to avoid buying random items.

questions.question_text: How does poor customer service affect a company's reputation in the age of social media?

chunks.text: Undeniably, bad service can destroy a brand instantly,
because negative reviews spread rapidly.
Consequently, a single viral complaint video can reach millions of potential buyers
in just a few hours.
For example, a rude waiter might cause an entire restaurant chain
to lose significant profits.

questions.question_text: Is the trend of automation (e.g., self-checkout) killing the "human touch" in shopping?

chunks.text: Admittedly, strict automation (自动化) reduces personal connections,
though it vastly improves speed.
Unfortunately, self-checkout machines cannot offer friendly conversations
or personalized recommendations to shoppers.
Therefore, elderly customers often feel socially isolated
when supermarkets replace all their human cashiers with screens.

questions.question_text:
Describe an online TV program. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: To start with, I want to describe a truly engaging (引人入胜的) documentary,
which is highly informative (信息量大的), and which focuses on wildlife.
Honestly, the production quality is absolutely impeccable (完美的).
Recently, it started to blow up (爆红) online.
As a result, I decided to binge-watch (刷剧) the entire season last weekend.
Actually, the short clips are so entertaining (有趣的 / 娱乐的)
that they often go viral (病毒式传播的 / 疯传的).
Furthermore, the storytelling is incredibly addictive (令人上瘾的).
Consequently, I found myself being very attentive (专注的) to every single detail.
Overall, I highly recommend that you scroll through (滑动浏览) the episodes and watch them.

questions.question_text:
Describe a short video you saw on social media that you enjoyed. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about a short cooking video
I saw on WeChat a few months ago.
To begin with, the video showed a woman making a simple noodle dish in under three minutes.
Honestly, it was so entertaining (有趣的 / 娱乐的) because she was funny and moved very fast.
The video was also really informative (信息量大的),
since she explained every step clearly.
I think watching her cook was almost addictive (令人上瘾的),
which made me want to keep watching more videos like it.
For example, I started to binge-watch (刷剧) her other cooking clips for over an hour.
The video had already gone viral (病毒式传播的 / 爆火的) before I even found it.
Of course, I shared it with my mum straight away
because she loves cooking and thought the recipe looked really easy to try at home.

questions.question_text:
Describe a TV series you recently started watching. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a TV series called *Squid Game*.
Actually, it is a Korean drama about people
who play dangerous games to win money.
To be honest, I first heard about it
when I started to scroll through (浏览 / 滑动 (手机)) my social media feed one evening
and saw everyone talking about it.
It had really blow up (突然爆红) online, so I got curious.
Honestly, the acting is impeccable (完美的) and every character feels very real.
I think the story is so engaging (引人入胜的)
that I always want to watch the next episode,
which means I sometimes stay up very late.
Furthermore, I stay very attentive (专注的) during each scene
because the story changes quickly.
In the end, this show is simply one of the best things I have watched recently.

questions.question_text: What do you think are the benefits of watching TV programs?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, television is an excellent stress reliever,
since it actively broadens our cultural horizons.
Specifically, the daily consumption (消费) of high-quality documentaries
teaches viewers about different global communities.
For example, a well-produced nature show
can significantly increase our awareness of critical environmental issues.

questions.question_text: Why don't young people pay much attention to local news?

chunks.text: Honestly, teenagers find local politics boring,
since they prefer global entertainment.
Furthermore, the mainstream (主流的) news networks rarely target
the interests of younger demographics.
Consequently, they would rather watch a funny video
go viral (疯传) on social media
than watch a city council meeting.

questions.question_text: Do you think some people waste too much time on social media?

chunks.text: Absolutely, smartphone addiction is a massive problem,
because it completely destroys daily productivity.
Undeniably, every social media algorithm (算法) is strictly designed
to keep users endlessly scrolling for hours.
As a result, many individuals become a lazy couch potato (电视迷 / 懒汉)
instead of going outside or exercising.

questions.question_text: What do you think are the drawbacks of social media?

chunks.text: In my view, although cyberbullying is a major issue,
false information is often much worse.
Sadly, the rapid online spread of misinformation (错误信息 / 谣言) creates unnecessary panic
and heavily divides communities.
For instance, malicious users frequently share dangerous fake news (假新闻)
during important political elections to manipulate voters.

questions.question_text: Has the rise of streaming services (like Netflix) destroyed traditional television?

chunks.text: Definitely, streaming platforms dominate the market,
while cable networks are dying.
Fundamentally, the automation (自动化) of video recommendations
gives viewers exactly what they want instantly.
Therefore, people prefer the connectivity (连接性) of watching shows on their phones
anytime over fixed broadcasting schedules.

questions.question_text: Do you think governments should censor content on the internet?

chunks.text: To an extent, regulating illegal content is necessary,
but strict control limits freedom.
Essentially, heavy censorship (审查) prevents citizens from accessing diverse global perspectives.
Instead, governments should promote digital literacy
so users can identify harmful content themselves
without relying on bans.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you had dinner together with your friend. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe a lively (热闹的) dinner party,
which was highly engaging (引人入胜的), and where the food was fantastic.
Honestly, the meal was absolutely delicious (美味的).
At first, the atmosphere felt slightly awkward (尴尬的)
because some guests didn't know each other.
However, my best friend managed to break the ice (打破僵局) with a funny story.
Actually, he is always very sociable (善于交际的) and the life of the party (派对的灵魂人物).
Furthermore, his jokes were incredibly entertaining (有趣的).
Ultimately, we all managed to have a blast (玩得很开心) together.
Overall, it was a truly memorable (难忘的) evening.

questions.question_text:
Describe a birthday party you attended that was fun. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about my friend Lisa's birthday party last year.
To be honest, it was one of the most memorable (难忘的) nights of my life.
The party was held at a small restaurant near our school.
To begin with, the place had a very lively (热闹的) atmosphere,
with music playing and colorful decorations everywhere.
Of course, the food was really delicious (美味的), and everyone enjoyed eating together.
Lisa is a very sociable (善于交际的) person,
so she introduced everyone and helped break the ice (打破僵局) between people who did not know each other.
I think the party was so enjoyable because Lisa made everyone feel welcome,
which helped us all relax and have a great time.
In the end, we danced, took many photos, and laughed a lot.
I really loved every moment of that evening.

questions.question_text:
Describe a family gathering you remember well. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: Actually, the family gathering I remember best
was my cousin's wedding dinner about three years ago.
To begin with, we all met at a big restaurant in my hometown,
and almost thirty family members came together.
Honestly, it felt a little awkward (尴尬的) at first
because some relatives hadn't seen each other for many years.
However, things quickly got better.
For example, my uncle is always the life of the party (聚会的灵魂人物),
so he told funny stories that made everyone laugh.
The evening was really entertaining (有趣的),
and we played some simple games between meals.
Furthermore, the conversations were so engaging (吸引人的)
that nobody wanted to leave early.
In the end, I think it was special
because we all got to have a blast (玩得很开心) together,
which made everyone feel close again,
and that feeling stayed with me for a long time.

questions.question_text: Why are people nowadays less willing to have meals with their families?

chunks.text: In modern society, since people are incredibly busy,
they frequently prioritize work over family.
Unfortunately, long commuting hours and stressful jobs
cause severe social isolation (孤立) within the household.
Consequently, young adults rarely share meaningful quality time (黄金时间)
with their parents at the dinner table anymore.

questions.question_text: What are the differences in food for different occasions?

chunks.text: Undeniably, daily meals are quick and simple,
whereas holiday feasts are highly complex and rich.
Fundamentally, any grand celebration (庆祝活动) requires special, expensive dishes
that deeply represent cultural tradition (传统).
For instance, Chinese families spend several days
preparing intricate dumplings and fish for the Lunar New Year dinner.

questions.question_text: What kind of music events do people like today?

chunks.text: Currently, huge outdoor festivals are incredibly popular,
because they offer highly diverse entertainment.
Specifically, these mainstream (主流的) events allow the youth
to securely let their hair down (尽情放松) and escape academic pressure.
For example, electronic dance music concerts attract thousands of energetic fans
every single summer weekend.

questions.question_text: Do you think children should receive some musical education?

chunks.text: Absolutely, learning a musical instrument is vital,
as it significantly boosts early brain development.
Furthermore, practicing classical music actively improves their neuronal connectivity (联系 / 连接性)
and emotional intelligence.
As an illustration, children who play the piano
often show much better focus and concentration
in their mathematics classes.

questions.question_text: Is it important for communities to have public festivals and celebrations?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, local festivals are essential,
since they unite different neighborhoods.
Essentially, a shared public event strengthens the community (社区 / 群体) bond,
proving that the more the merrier (人越多越热闹).
For instance, a summer food market encourages strangers to mingle
and build local friendships safely.

questions.question_text: How has the definition of "socializing" changed with the introduction of smartphones?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, because socializing is now mostly digital,
it lacks genuine intimacy.
Nowadays, strict physical etiquette (礼节) is ignored
because people prefer texting over talking face-to-face.
Consequently, groups of friends often sit together in cafes
while silently staring at their individual screens.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person who apologized to you. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about my highly sociable (善交际的) colleague,
who is generally very honest (诚实的), but who made a mistake.
Honestly, the situation was incredibly awkward (尴尬的) at first.
Actually, he lost an important file,
but he was very courageous (勇敢的) to admit it.
Furthermore, he sounded truly regretful (后悔的).
Ultimately, he decided to swallow his pride (放下自尊) and apologize.
Moreover, he just wanted to get something off his chest (一吐为快 / 倾吐心声).
Consequently, his sincere (真诚的) apology managed to clear the air (消除误会) between us completely.
Finally, I am a very forgiving (宽容的) person, so we moved on quickly.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you made a mistake and had to say sorry. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about a time
when I forgot my best friend's birthday.
To be honest, I was very busy with exams
and I completely forgot the date.
She felt really hurt,
and I could see she was upset.
Actually, I felt so regretful (后悔的) when I saw her sad face.
I decided to swallow one's pride (放下自尊) and go to her home
to say sorry in person.
I brought her a small cake
and wrote a card to show I was sincere (真诚的).
I told her the truth
because I wanted to be honest (诚实的) about why I forgot.
Honestly, I think she is a very forgiving (宽容的) person,
because she smiled and said it was okay,
which made me feel much better.
In the end, our friendship became even stronger.

questions.question_text:
Describe a disagreement you had with a close friend that was resolved. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a disagreement I had with my best friend, Li Wei.
He is very sociable (善交际的 (but made mistake)) and friendly,
but one day he told our other friends about a personal problem
I had shared with him privately.
To be honest, I felt really hurt and angry.
For a few days, things were very awkward (尴尬的) between us.
Actually, I knew I needed to be courageous (勇敢的) and talk to him directly.
So I called him and decided to get something off one's chest (吐露心声 / 一吐为快) —
I told him honestly how his actions had made me feel.
He said sorry right away,
and we were able to clear the air (消除误会)
because we both wanted to stay friends,
which made our friendship feel even stronger than before.
In the end, I felt much better and happy.

questions.question_text: In what situations should we apologize immediately?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, we must apologize quickly when we cause physical harm,
or when we accidentally damage property.
Fundamentally, promptly acknowledging a mistake prevents a minor accident
from escalating into a serious emotional conflict (冲突).
For instance, if you accidentally spill hot coffee on a colleague,
an immediate apology is essential for harmony.

questions.question_text: Do you think accepting apologies is always the correct thing to do?

chunks.text: In my opinion, forgiveness is generally good,
but it depends on the severity of the offense.
Specifically, if someone displays malicious deception (欺骗),
blindly accepting their apology is foolish.
Therefore, while it is nice to bury the hatchet (握手言和),
repeated betrayals do not deserve instant forgiveness.

questions.question_text: Do you agree that sometimes people should tell lies (white lies)?

chunks.text: Admittedly, strict truthfulness is ideal, yet small fabrications are occasionally completely necessary.
Sometimes, basic social etiquette (礼节) requires a harmless white lie (善意的谎言) to actively protect someone's fragile feelings.
For example, pretending to love a terrible birthday gift prevents unnecessary sadness
and maintains the long-term friendship.

questions.question_text: Do you think we should tell the truth at all times?

chunks.text: Ultimately, I firmly believe that honesty is the best policy (诚实是上策) in almost all situations.
Essentially, personal integrity (正直 / 诚信) builds trust,
which is the foundation of any healthy relationship.
Therefore, constantly lying, even about small things,
will eventually destroy a person's credibility and reputation completely.

questions.question_text: Is "honesty" still valued as a primary virtue in modern business?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, profit often overshadows truth,
though ethical companies still prioritize transparency.
Today, corporate morality (道德) is frequently compromised
by aggressive marketing and hidden fees.
However, brands that maintain absolute honesty
usually secure much stronger, long-term loyalty
from their dedicated customer base.

questions.question_text: How does a culture of forgiveness contribute to social harmony?

chunks.text: Undeniably, since forgiveness reduces societal tension,
it effectively promotes peaceful coexistence.
Basically, without a clear path for conflict resolution (解决),
grudges would divide a local community (社区) permanently.
As an illustration, restorative justice programs allow offenders to make amends,
thereby healing the neighborhood collectively.

questions.question_text:
Describe a wild animal that you want to know more about. (Examiner Note: You may focus on where this animal lives) You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe the tiger,
which is a truly fascinating (迷人的) creature,
and which is sadly endangered (濒危的) today.
Honestly, it is a completely wild (野生的) animal that lives in deep forests.
Actually, I dream of seeing one face to face (面对面) in a nature reserve.
Furthermore, they look incredibly majestic (雄伟的).
Consequently, I have a sincere (真诚的) interest in their unique (独特的) hunting habits.
Moreover, observing them in the wild (在野外) requires a courageous (勇敢的) heart.
Ultimately, the tiger is known as the king of the jungle (丛林之王).
Overall, I want to learn more to help protect them.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time you visited a zoo or wildlife park. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about a trip I took to the Beijing Zoo about two years ago.
To be honest, I went there with my younger sister and our parents on a sunny weekend.
To begin with, we walked around and saw many different animals.
For example, we saw pandas, monkeys, and some endangered (濒危的) animals like snow leopards.
I think the most fascinating (迷人的) part was standing face to face (面对面) with a giant panda,
which made me feel really happy because it was so close to me.
Of course, seeing the lions was also amazing —
they looked so majestic (雄伟的 / 威严的) and powerful.
Furthermore, I learned that many wild (野生的) animals are losing their homes,
and I felt a little sad about that.
In the end, it was a really good day,
and I hope to visit again soon.

questions.question_text:
Describe a nature documentary about animals that you watched. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a nature documentary I watched
called "African Cats."
To begin with, it was about lions and cheetahs
living in the wild (在野外) in Africa.
Actually, I watched it at home on my laptop
one evening with my family.
Honestly, it was a really unique (独特的) film
because it followed real animal families,
which made me feel very close to the animals.
For example, I learned that the lion,
known as the king of the jungle (丛林之王 (狮子)),
works very hard to protect its family.
I also saw how courageous (勇敢的 (to approach)) a mother cheetah can be
when she fights for her babies.
In the end, the film left a strong impression on me
because it gave me a sincere (真诚的 (interest)) interest in protecting wild animals
and their homes.

questions.question_text: Why are some people more willing to protect wild animals than others?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, education plays a huge role,
since some people truly understand global biodiversity (生物多样性).
Specifically, those who study nature realize
that preventing animal extinction (灭绝) is crucial for human survival too.
For example, individuals working in global conservation (保护) deeply value every single species.

questions.question_text: Do you think it's important to take children to the zoo to see animals?

chunks.text: In my opinion, visiting zoos is highly educational,
though keeping animals in captivity (圈养) remains controversial today.
Fundamentally, seeing real creatures builds empathy,
which can prevent future human-animal conflict (冲突).
Therefore, children who watch lions closely learn to respect nature deeply
rather than fearing it.

questions.question_text: Does tourism have a positive or negative impact on animals?

chunks.text: Honestly, it is a double-edged sword,
because it funds parks but ruins habitats.
Unfortunately, heavy foot traffic frequently disrupts a delicate ecosystem (生态系统) completely.
To illustrate, many businesses shed crocodile tears (鳄鱼的眼泪) about protecting wildlife
while secretly destroying forests for new hotels.

questions.question_text: Should animals be kept in captivity for entertainment purposes?

chunks.text: Absolutely not, using animals for cheap amusement
completely violates basic human morality (道德).
Essentially, forcing wild beasts to perform unnatural tricks in circuses
is cruel and outdated.
Consequently, it is much better to let sleeping dogs lie (莫惹是非)
and leave animals undisturbed.

questions.question_text: How does the extinction of a single species affect the wider environment?

chunks.text: Undeniably, losing one species creates a massive chain reaction,
which damages food webs.
In nature, the harsh law of survival of the fittest (适者生存)
maintains a very delicate environmental balance.
As a result, if bees vanish,
countless plants and other dependent animals will also die rapidly.

questions.question_text: Is it the responsibility of rich nations to help poor nations protect their wildlife?

chunks.text: Certainly, wealthy countries must provide funding,
since environmental protection is a global duty.
Actually, poorer nations often lack the necessary financial resources
to fight illegal poaching effectively.
For instance, donating advanced tracking technology
helps developing countries save rare rhinos from armed hunters.

questions.question_text:
Describe an area of science (physics, biology, psychology, etc.) that interests you. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about astronomy,
which is highly complex (复杂的), and which explores the universe.
Honestly, it is a truly fascinating (迷人的) subject for me.
Furthermore, understanding black holes requires a very logical (合乎逻辑的) and theoretical (理论的) approach.
Actually, reading about space gives me endless food for thought (引人深思的事).
Sometimes, the vastness of galaxies gives me a light bulb moment (顿悟时刻) about human existence.
Moreover, astronomers are doing groundbreaking (开创性的) and creative (有创造力的) work every day.
Ultimately, studying this unique (独特的) field is not exactly rocket science (高深莫测的事) if you have passion.

questions.question_text:
Describe a book or documentary that taught you something new about the world. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about a documentary called *Cosmos*,
which I watched about two years ago.
To begin with, it was about the universe
and how everything in space works.
Honestly, I thought science was always hard to understand,
but this documentary made it feel simple.
For example, the host explained that space is actually very complex (复杂的) but also beautiful.
Actually, I learned that stars can die
and then become new planets,
which I found truly fascinating (迷人的).
It was also very logical (合乎逻辑的),
because each idea connected to the next one clearly.
Furthermore, the way they showed space pictures was so creative (有创造力的) and easy to follow.
In the end, I think understanding the universe
is not exactly rocket science (高深学问) when someone explains it well,
which made me feel excited because learning became fun.

questions.question_text:
Describe a subject you studied at school that you found very interesting. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: Actually, the subject I want to talk about is Physics,
which I studied in high school.
To be honest, it was my favourite class at that time.
My teacher, Mr. Chen, was really good at explaining difficult ideas in a simple way.
To begin with, I loved how the lessons mixed theoretical (理论的) ideas with real-life examples.
For example, learning about electricity gave me a true light bulb moment (顿悟时刻)
because I finally understood how everyday things around me actually worked.
Furthermore, Mr. Chen always shared groundbreaking (开创性的) discoveries from famous scientists,
which made every lesson feel exciting.
Honestly, Physics gave me so much food for thought (引人深思的事)
that I often kept thinking about it after class.
I think Physics was meaningful to me
because it showed me a unique (独特的) way to see the world,
which changed how I think about everything.

questions.question_text: Are science majors popular in schools?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, STEM subjects are highly popular,
because they offer excellent career prospects.
Specifically, students with natural curiosity (好奇心)
are drawn to fields that drive global innovation (创新).
For example, computer science degrees are currently
the most sought-after programs in many modern universities.

questions.question_text: Is learning science important? What are the applications of science in daily life?

chunks.text: Absolutely, scientific knowledge is crucial,
as it builds the foundation of modern society.
In fact, every medical breakthrough (突破) relies heavily on rigorous testing
and repeated experiment (实验).
Therefore, the medicines we take daily are direct results of careful biological research.

questions.question_text: Which requires more imagination: reading books or watching cartoons?

chunks.text: In my opinion, reading demands much more imagination,
since the visuals are completely absent.
Essentially, when reading text, your brain must actively construct every scene
instead of having it handed to you.
As an illustration, two people reading the same novel
will picture the characters entirely differently,
showing that great minds think alike (英雄所见略同) but imagine uniquely.

questions.question_text: Which occupations (or jobs) require a high degree of imagination?

chunks.text: Undeniably, architects and writers need immense creativity,
but scientists do too.
Fundamentally, researchers must formulate a unique hypothesis (假设)
before they can discover anything new.
Consequently, an ecologist studying a complex ecosystem (生态系统)
must imagine unseen connections to solve environmental puzzles.

questions.question_text: Does the current education system encourage or kill creativity?

chunks.text: Sadly, rigid schooling often stifles creativity,
because it focuses heavily on standardized testing.
Unfortunately, students are taught to memorize facts
rather than explore new ideas,
forcing them to reinvent the wheel (多此一举) unnecessarily.
For instance, strict grading rubrics penalize students
who solve math problems using unconventional methods.

questions.question_text: Is scientific progress always good for society, or are there ethical risks?

chunks.text: Admittedly, progress brings huge benefits,
yet it also carries severe ethical dangers.
Sometimes, corporations try to blind with science (故弄玄虚)
to hide the devastating environmental impacts of their inventions.
Therefore, unregulated genetic modification might eventually lead
to the terrifying extinction (灭绝) of natural plant species.

questions.question_text:
Describe a new activity that you tried for the first time and felt excited about. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about indoor rock climbing,
which is incredibly complex (复杂的), and which requires great strength.
Honestly, the steep learning curve (学习曲线) made it incredibly challenging (充满挑战的) at first.
Actually, looking down from the wall was extremely nerve-wracking (令人紧张的).
However, I was highly determined (坚定的) to reach the top.
Furthermore, my friend encouraged me to just give it a go (试一试).
Eventually, I managed to get the hang of it (掌握窍门)
by using a very logical (合乎逻辑的) climbing technique.
Ultimately, the feeling of success was absolutely exhilarating (令人兴奋的).
Overall, it was a deeply rewarding (有回报的) experience.

questions.question_text:
Describe a skill that took you a long time to learn. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about learning to drive.
Honestly, I started learning when I was about twenty years old.
To begin with, it felt very nerve-wracking (令人紧张的) because there were so many things to think about at the same time.
Actually, my teacher told me to just give it a go (试一试) every day,
even when I made mistakes.
Of course, it was really challenging (充满挑战的) to control the car smoothly in busy traffic,
which meant I had to practice for almost one full year before I passed my test.
Furthermore, I remember feeling very exhilarating (令人兴奋的) on the day I finally passed.
