In the end, all those hours of hard work made the whole experience truly rewarding (值得的 / 有回报的).
I think this skill gave me real freedom and a lot of confidence in my daily life.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you helped someone learn something new. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a time
when I helped my younger cousin learn how to use Excel
for her school project.
To begin with, she had no idea how to work with formulas,
and she felt really lost.
Honestly, she was very determined (下定决心的) to learn,
so I was happy to help her.
I used a logical (合乎逻辑的 (approach)) approach
and taught her step by step,
starting with simple things first.
For example, I showed her that Excel can look complex (复杂的),
but it becomes easier with practice.
Of course, there was a learning curve (学习曲线 (上手难度)),
and she made many small mistakes early on.
In the end, she managed to get the hang of it (掌握窍门)
because I gave her lots of examples to try,
which made her feel more confident.
I felt really proud and happy after that experience.

questions.question_text: Why do some people like extreme sports?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, thrill-seekers love the massive adrenaline rush,
because it makes them feel truly alive.
Furthermore, conquering a terrifying physical barrier (障碍)
provides a deep sense of personal achievement.
For instance, skydiving forces people to face their darkest fears,
rapidly increasing their mental adaptability (适应能力).

questions.question_text: What common activities could make people relax?

chunks.text: In my opinion, reading books and doing yoga are fantastic ways to unwind completely.
Basically, these quiet hobbies allow individuals to escape the intense pressure
of constant workplace innovation (创新).
Therefore, spending an hour meditating in a silent room
lowers stress and restores mental peace instantly.

questions.question_text: Why do people learn foreign languages?

chunks.text: Undeniably, learning languages is essential for career growth,
and it significantly helps people travel easily.
Due to rapid globalization (全球化),
international companies demand employees with high linguistic proficiency (熟练程度).
Consequently, mastering English or Spanish allows young professionals
to successfully broaden their horizons (开阔眼界).

questions.question_text: At what age do you think is the best time for people to learn a foreign language?

chunks.text: Absolutely, early childhood is the optimal period,
since young brains absorb information like sponges.
Essentially, language acquisition (习得) is much faster before the age of ten
because children possess endless curiosity (好奇心).
For example, toddlers raised in bilingual households
learn to speak two languages without any conscious effort.

questions.question_text: Is it necessary to learn the culture of a country when learning its language?

chunks.text: Definitely, understanding the culture is crucial,
because language is deeply tied to social history.
In fact, to truly learn the ropes (摸清门道) of local slang,
you must understand the cultural context behind the words.
As an illustration, Japanese greetings change significantly
depending on the strict social hierarchy and politeness levels.

questions.question_text: Does globalization mean that everyone will eventually speak the same language?

chunks.text: Personally, I highly doubt it,
although English will likely remain the dominant business tool.
Ultimately, people value their native tongues too much to abandon them entirely,
even though practice makes perfect (熟能生巧) in English.
Therefore, regional dialects and historical languages will always survive
alongside any global lingua franca.

questions.question_text:
Describe a good habit your friend has and you want to develop. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about my highly disciplined (自律的) friend,
who is incredibly productive (高效的), and who always wakes up early.
Honestly, his daily schedule is extremely beneficial (有益的) for his health.
Actually, I am naturally a creature of habit (墨守成规的人) who stays up late,
which is detrimental (有害的).
However, I am determined (坚定的) to turn over a new leaf (改头换面).
Furthermore, I really want to kick the habit (戒除恶习) of scrolling on my phone at night.
Consequently, if I remain persistent (坚持不懈的),
waking up early will be deeply rewarding (有回报的).
Overall, I admire his excellent time management.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person you know who changed a bad habit successfully. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about my close friend, Li Wei.
Honestly, he used to stay up very late every night playing video games.
To be honest, this habit was really detrimental (有害的) to his health,
and he often felt tired at work.
However, one day he decided to turn over a new leaf (改过自新 / 重新开始) and make some big changes.
To begin with, he set a strict sleep time and deleted his gaming apps.
Actually, he became very disciplined (自律的) and followed his new routine every day.
I think his life improved a lot
because he started sleeping well,
which made him more productive (高效的 / 多产的) at his job.
Furthermore, eating better food was also very beneficial (有益的) for his body.
In the end, he looked happier and healthier than before.

questions.question_text:
Describe a daily routine that you think is very important. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: Honestly, the daily routine I want to talk about is morning exercise.
To begin with, I started this habit about two years ago
when I decided to kick the habit (戒除恶习) of staying in bed too long every morning.
Actually, I am a real creature of habit (墨守成规的人),
so once I started, it became very natural for me.
I think this routine is important
because it makes my body and mind feel strong.
Furthermore, I had to be very determined (下定决心的) in the beginning,
especially on cold days.
To be honest, I feel this routine has been truly rewarding (有回报的)
because my energy levels are much higher now,
which means I can focus better at school.
In the end, being persistent (坚持不懈的) with this small habit
has really changed my life in a positive way.

questions.question_text: What influences do children with bad habits have on other children?

chunks.text: Undeniably, negative peer influence (影响) is highly contagious,
because kids mimic each other constantly.
Unfortunately, children lack the necessary willpower (意志力) to resist copying bad behavior
like swearing or skipping homework.
As a result, one disruptive student can easily ruin the entire classroom's focus
and academic proficiency (熟练程度).

questions.question_text: Why do some habits change when people get older?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, age brings maturity,
and aging bodies demand a healthier lifestyle (生活方式).
Although old habits die hard (积习难改),
severe health scares often force adults to adjust their toxic patterns.
For instance, a person might finally stop smoking
after realizing the deadly consequence (后果) on their lungs.

questions.question_text: How do we develop bad habits?

chunks.text: Essentially, poor choices become automatic,
because humans naturally seek instant gratification.
Specifically, the rapid acquisition (养成) of a terrible routine (常规)
usually begins as a temporary coping mechanism for daily stress.
Therefore, eating sugary chocolate once to feel better
quickly turns into a daily sugar addiction.

questions.question_text: What can we do to get rid of bad habits?

chunks.text: In my opinion, substituting the bad action with a healthy alternative is highly effective.
Frankly, relying purely on motivation fails;
you must consciously change your environment to succeed.
As an illustration, an early bird (早起的人) who wants to stop drinking coffee
should drink green tea instead.

questions.question_text: Do you think successful people always have strict daily routines?

chunks.text: Mostly yes, top performers thrive on structure,
though some creative geniuses prefer absolute chaos.
Normally, having a predictable schedule saves mental energy
for making extremely important business decisions.
However, some famous artists are deeply set in their ways (固执己见)
and refuse any strict timetables completely.

questions.question_text: Is self-discipline more important than natural talent for success?

chunks.text: Absolutely, hard work consistently beats raw talent,
especially when talent is lazy.
Fundamentally, unyielding discipline guarantees continuous improvement,
whereas natural gifts eventually plateau without strict practice.
For example, a highly disciplined athlete will eventually outrun a naturally fast runner
who skips daily training.

questions.question_text:
Describe an occasion when you were not allowed to use your mobile phone. (Examiner Note: Focus your description on the place/setting where this rule applied) You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To start with, I want to talk about my university library,
which is incredibly strict (严格的),
and where using phones is entirely prohibited (禁止的).
Honestly, the librarians are very disciplined (守纪律的) about this policy.
Furthermore, any loud noise is considered highly disruptive (破坏性的 / 扰乱的) to studying students.
Consequently, they never bend the rules (变通) for anyone.
Therefore, I always make sure to follow the rules (遵守规则) carefully.
Actually, being a respectful (尊重的) and considerate (体贴的) student is important here.
Moreover, distractions are completely detrimental (有害的) to learning.
Ultimately, I just keep my voice down (压低声音) to avoid trouble.

questions.question_text:
Describe a public place where people must be quiet. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about the library near my university.
To begin with, it is a large, quiet building with many books and study rooms.
I go there about two or three times a week.
Honestly, silence is very important there because students need to focus on their work,
which means any loud noise can be really disruptive (破坏性的 / 扰乱的) and make it hard for everyone to study.
Of course, talking loudly is completely prohibited (禁止的) inside.
The rules are quite strict (严格的),
and staff will ask you to leave if you make noise.
Actually, I always try to follow the rules (遵守规则) when I am there.
For example, I turn my phone to silent mode before I enter.
I think being respectful (尊重的) to other people around you
is just a basic thing to do in a shared space.

questions.question_text:
Describe a rule at your school or workplace that you think is very important. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: Actually, the rule I want to talk about
is the quiet rule in our school library.
To begin with, everyone must keep one's voice down (小声说话) at all times inside.
This rule exists because loud noise is very detrimental (有害的)
to students who are trying to study or concentrate.
Honestly, I think it also helps everyone feel more comfortable.
If someone breaks this rule, a librarian will give them a warning,
and they may have to leave the room.
Of course, some people try to bend the rules (通融 / 变通) by whispering loudly,
but most students understand the importance.
I totally agree with this rule
because a good student should be disciplined (守纪律的) and respect others around them.
Furthermore, being considerate (体贴的) of other people's needs makes the library a better place for everyone,
which helps the whole school community.

questions.question_text: How do young and old people use mobile phones differently?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, teenagers use phones for entertainment,
whereas seniors mainly use them for calling.
Specifically, young people love the freedom (自由) of social media,
which exerts a massive influence (影响) on their daily lives.
For instance, an elderly person might just check the weather,
while a student watches hours of videos.

questions.question_text: Do you think it is necessary to have laws on the use of mobile phones?

chunks.text: Absolutely, strict regulations are crucial,
especially when public safety is directly involved.
Fundamentally, maintaining basic law and order (法律与秩序)
requires firm restriction (限制) on distracted driving.
Therefore, enforcing steep fines ensures driver compliance (服从)
and prevents terrible accidents on the highway.

questions.question_text: Is it a waste of time to take pictures with mobile phones?

chunks.text: In my view, taking a few photos is wonderful,
but constantly snapping pictures ruins the moment.
Unfortunately, some tourists completely cross the line (越界) by blocking pathways just to get a selfie.
As a result, they forget basic common courtesy (基本礼貌)
and annoy everyone else around them.

questions.question_text: Should there be more "phone-free zones" in public spaces?

chunks.text: Definitely, establishing quiet areas is a great idea,
because it forces people to disconnect.
Actually, proper social etiquette (礼节) is often forgotten
in busy restaurants or cinemas.
For example, a phone-free cafe allows customers
to enjoy genuine conversations without any digital interruptions.

questions.question_text: How has constant connectivity (always being online) affected mental health?

chunks.text: Sadly, being always online increases anxiety,
and it disrupts normal sleeping patterns.
Essentially, the negative consequence (后果) of checking notifications at night
is chronic exhaustion.
Consequently, many teenagers suffer from severe stress
because they feel pressured to reply to messages instantly.

questions.question_text: Do rules in society limit our freedom or protect us?

chunks.text: Ultimately, well-designed laws protect citizens,
even though they impose minor daily limits.
Basically, without a strong authority (权威) enforcing safety guidelines,
chaos would easily destroy communities.
To illustrate, traffic lights technically restrict movement,
but they prevent fatal collisions at busy intersections.

questions.question_text:
Describe something you plan regularly. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about planning my weekly study timetable,
which keeps me organized (有条理的), and which prevents me from feeling anxious (焦虑的).
Honestly, my school life is incredibly hectic (忙乱的).
Therefore, I try to plan ahead (提前计划) every Sunday evening.
Actually, I am very strict (严格的) about being punctual (守时的) for classes.
Sometimes, I get impatient (不耐烦的) if unexpected events are disruptive (扰乱的) to my routine.
During breaks, I just read a book to kill time (消磨时间).
Ultimately, getting good grades makes this planning effort worth the wait (值得等待).

questions.question_text:
Describe a time when you had to wait a long time for something. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about a time
when I waited for my university exam results.
Honestly, the wait was almost two weeks,
which felt very long.
To begin with, the exam period was really hectic (忙乱的),
so I was happy when it finished.
However, after the exams, I became very anxious (焦虑的) about my scores.
I tried to kill time (消磨时间) by watching videos
and going for walks with friends.
To be honest, I also tried to stay organized (有条理的) during the wait
by making a study plan for the next term.
I think waiting is hard for people
who are very punctual (守时的) and like things to happen on time,
which made me realize that I need to be more patient,
and that good things are worth waiting for.
In the end, my results were good,
so I felt very happy.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person you know who is very good at managing their time. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about my older sister.
She is a university teacher,
and she wakes up at six every morning.
To begin with, she follows a very strict (严格的 (schedule)) daily routine,
so she never feels rushed.
Actually, she always plan ahead (未雨绸缪) for the whole week on Sunday evening.
For example, she writes down every task and meeting in a notebook.
Honestly, she is never impatient (不耐烦的) when things take time,
because she builds extra time into her schedule.
She told me that any disruptive (扰乱的 (delay)) moment,
like a late bus or a cancelled class,
cannot really hurt her plans.
I think good preparation is always worth the wait (值得等待),
because the results are better,
which means less stress in everyday life.
In the end, I learned that planning carefully makes everything much easier.

questions.question_text: Is making plans important?

chunks.text: Absolutely, setting clear goals is crucial,
because it gives people direction in life.
Essentially, strong time management (管理) prevents terrible procrastination (拖延症)
and lowers daily stress.
For instance, writing a simple to-do list helps students finish their homework much faster.

questions.question_text: Should children plan their future careers?

chunks.text: Definitely, teenagers should start thinking about jobs early,
but they should remain flexible.
Ultimately, making education a top priority (优先事项) helps them choose the right university courses later.
However, since time flies (光阴似箭) and industries change quickly,
their plans will naturally evolve.

questions.question_text: Do you think it is important to be a patient person?

chunks.text: Without a doubt, staying calm is vital,
since rushing often leads to careless mistakes.
In fact, true efficiency (效率) requires a steady pace
rather than frantic panic.
Therefore, doctors must remain completely patient during a long surgery
to ensure the patient's safety.

questions.question_text: Why do some people dislike waiting?

chunks.text: Honestly, people hate delays,
because they feel their precious time is being stolen.
Specifically, a strict restriction (限制) on someone's daily schedule (日程安排)
causes extreme frustration.
For example, commuters get very angry
when a late train makes them miss an important morning meeting.

questions.question_text: Does modern technology make us more impatient?

chunks.text: Admittedly, fast internet makes us expect instant results,
which lowers our tolerance for delays.
Consequently, when a web page takes ten seconds to load,
we struggle with compliance (顺从 / 遵守) and get annoyed.
As a result, people frequently try to beat the clock (赶时间)
instead of enjoying the current moment.

questions.question_text: "Time is money." Do you agree with this statement? Why or why not?

chunks.text: To a large extent, I agree,
because wasted hours equal lost earning opportunities.
However, resting is also valuable,
so perhaps we should say better late than never (迟做总比不做好)
when finding balance.
For instance, working constantly creates wealth,
but skipping sleep will eventually destroy your health.

questions.question_text:
Describe a competition (e.g., TV, school, or sports) that you took part in. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: First of all, I want to describe a school debate tournament,
which was highly prestigious (有声望的),
and where the atmosphere was incredibly intense (激烈的).
Honestly, I felt quite anxious (焦虑的) beforehand.
Actually, the event was very well organized (有组织的).
Furthermore, my opponents were extremely competitive (竞争激烈的 / 好胜的).
However, I decided to give it my all (全力以赴)
because I wanted to be in it to win it (志在必得).
Consequently, I practiced my speech for weeks.
Surprisingly, our team managed to win hands down (轻而易举地获胜).
Ultimately, feeling victorious (胜利的) and triumphant (欢欣鼓舞的) on stage was simply amazing.

questions.question_text:
Describe a prize or award that you won. You should say:
what it is
how you got it or learned about it
how you use it or why it matters
and explain why it is important to you

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about a writing competition
I won in my second year of high school.
To begin with, it was a prestigious (享有声望的) city-level essay contest,
and many schools took part.
Honestly, the atmosphere was very intense (激烈的).
I wrote about protecting the environment,
and I tried my best to share real feelings in my writing.
To be honest, I was quite competitive (好胜的 / 竞争激烈的),
so I practiced writing every day before the contest.
I was competing against over two hundred students from different schools,
so I didn't think I could win hands down (赢得轻松).
In the end, I felt so proud and happy when I heard my name called,
because I knew all my hard work finally paid off,
which made me feel truly victorious (胜利的).
It was a wonderful memory.

questions.question_text:
Describe a live sports match or competition you watched. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about a football match I watched last year
at a local stadium.
To begin with, it was a city championship,
and it was very well organized (有组织的 (event)) — there were thousands of fans there.
Honestly, both teams seemed to really give it your all (全力以赴) from the very first minute.
I remember feeling quite anxious (焦虑的) because the score was tied for a long time.
Actually, it was clear that every player was be in it to win it (志在必得),
and nobody wanted to lose.
In the end, the home team scored in the final minute,
which made the whole crowd go crazy,
and the players looked completely triumphant (欢欣鼓舞的 (胜利)) when the final whistle blew.
I think that match was so exciting
because the result was unknown until the very last second.

questions.question_text: Why do people enjoy competitions or contests?

chunks.text: Generally, people enjoy contests, because they love testing their limits against others.
Fundamentally, a healthy rivalry (竞争 / 敌对) provides immense motivation
and boosts personal determination (决心).
For example, runners often achieve their fastest times
during a real race rather than during lonely practice sessions.

questions.question_text: Is it good to teach children to be competitive at a young age?

chunks.text: In my opinion, mild competition is fine,
but intense pressure hurts young minds.
Essentially, kids need to learn good sportsmanship (体育精神)
rather than obsessing over winning.
Therefore, emphasizing fun creates a win-win situation (双赢局面)
where every child feels valued regardless of the final score.

questions.question_text: What skills can people learn from team sports that they cannot learn from individual sports?

chunks.text: Undeniably, group sports teach incredible teamwork (团队合作),
which individual sports simply cannot provide.
Specifically, players learn strict cooperation (合作) and trust
by relying on their peers.
For instance, a soccer player must pass the ball unselfishly
to secure a victory for the whole squad.

questions.question_text: Do you think companies encourage competition between employees? Is this healthy?

chunks.text: Admittedly, many businesses push internal contests,
yet this often ruins office harmony.
Actually, if management (管理) makes sales targets the only priority (优先事项),
employees might sabotage each other.
Consequently, it is usually much healthier to reward collective team success
rather than praising cut-throat individuals.

questions.question_text: Why are reality TV competitions (like singing or cooking shows) so popular globally?

chunks.text: Honestly, television contests are engaging,
because viewers love dramatic human stories.
Furthermore, watching ordinary people compete on a level playing field (公平竞争的环境)
is deeply inspiring.
As an illustration, a singing show allows an unknown amateur
to become a global superstar overnight.

questions.question_text: Is "winning" the only thing that matters in professional sports?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, winning is heavily prioritized,
though fan entertainment is also crucial.
Essentially, professional athletes must constantly keep their eye on the ball (保持专注) to secure lucrative contracts.
As a result, a football coach will quickly be fired
if his team loses multiple games,
despite playing beautifully.

questions.question_text:
Describe a neighbor you have who is interesting or helpful. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to talk about my next-door neighbor (隔壁邻居),
who is incredibly neighborly (睦邻友好的), and who always smiles.
Honestly, he is a very helpful (乐于助人的) and generous (慷慨的) elderly man.
Actually, some people in our building are quite nosy (爱管闲事的)
or overly competitive (爱攀比的).
However, he is just remarkably considerate (体贴的) without any intense (强烈的) drama.
For instance, he will gladly keep an eye on (照看) my cat when I travel.
Furthermore, he is always ready to lend a hand (伸出援手) with heavy groceries.
Ultimately, having someone so reliable living nearby is a huge blessing.

questions.question_text:
Describe a person who does a lot of good things for your local community. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about Mr. Chen,
an older man who lives near my home.
To begin with, he is very neighborly (睦邻友好的)
and always says hello to everyone on the street.
Honestly, he is one of the most generous (慷慨的) people I know,
because he often buys food for families who don't have much money.
He also likes to lend a hand (伸出援手) when people need help
moving furniture or fixing things.
Of course, some people think he is a little nosy (爱管闲事的 (negative))
because he always knows what is happening in the area,
but I think his heart is good.
I believe he does all this because he truly cares about people.
He is so helpful (乐于助人的) that our street feels safer and warmer,
which makes everyone happy to live there.

questions.question_text:
Describe a time you helped a neighbor with a problem. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: Actually, I want to talk about the time I helped my next-door neighbor (隔壁邻居), Mrs. Chen, last winter.
To begin with, she had a small water leak in her kitchen,
and she did not know how to fix it.
Honestly, she felt very worried because she lives alone.
I think I am quite considerate (体贴的),
so I called a plumber for her and stayed with her the whole afternoon.
Furthermore, I agreed to keep an eye on (照看 / 留意) her apartment for the next few days
to make sure everything was okay.
In the end, the problem was fixed quickly,
and she was very happy.
To be honest, I felt an intense (强烈的 (dislike)) sense of satisfaction after helping her.
Our neighborhood is sometimes competitive (爱攀比的),
but moments like this remind me that being kind to others is more important.

questions.question_text: What are the qualities of a "good neighbor"?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, a good neighbor is quiet,
and they respect boundaries.
In fact, the old saying good fences make good neighbors (保持距离才能和睦相处)
highlights the deep need for personal privacy.
Therefore, being polite but not overly intrusive
creates the most peaceful living environment.

questions.question_text: How has the relationship between neighbors changed compared to the past?

chunks.text: Undeniably, people are much less connected now,
because they live busier lives.
Historically, close village cooperation (合作) was essential for survival,
but modern convenience has ruined that.
Consequently, today's rapid urbanization (城市化) means people often do not even know
the names of people living next door.

questions.question_text: Why do people in big cities often not know their neighbors?

chunks.text: Mainly, high-rise apartments create physical barriers,
which severely limit daily interaction (互动).
Furthermore, the fast pace of city life
creates a sad sense of social isolation (孤立).
For instance, commuters arrive home exhausted
and immediately lock their doors
instead of chatting in the hallway.

questions.question_text: Do you think community centers are important for a neighborhood?

chunks.text: Absolutely, local centers are incredibly important,
as they provide a shared gathering space.
Essentially, they help build a strong community (社区)
by hosting events that are close to home (切身相关的).
As an illustration, a local hall offering free dance classes
helps strangers become fast friends.

questions.question_text: How can neighbors help each other in times of crisis or emergency?

chunks.text: During disasters, neighbors are the first responders,
because they are physically closest.
Fundamentally, when a storm hits,
people must drop any petty rivalry (敌对)
and quickly look out for each other (守望相助).
For example, sharing emergency food supplies
and checking on elderly residents
ensures everyone's basic security (安全).

questions.question_text: Should there be laws to control noisy neighbors?

chunks.text: Definitely, noise regulations are absolutely necessary,
since constant loud sounds ruin mental health.
Actually, everyone deserves a quiet place to sleep
after a long, exhausting day of work.
Therefore, police should be able to fine people
who blast loud music at two in the morning.

questions.question_text:
Describe a place near water (such as a river, lake, or the ocean) that you enjoyed visiting. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe a serene (宁静的) bay,
which is incredibly breathtaking (美得惊人的),
and which I visited last summer.
Honestly, the water there is absolutely crystal clear (清澈见底)
and perfectly safe for swimming.
Furthermore, the local people are extremely neighborly (友好的) and welcoming to all tourists.
Actually, the beach is just a stone's throw away (近在咫尺) from a very comfortable hotel.
During the day, I simply loved to soak up the sun (沐浴阳光) on the warm sand.
Moreover, the cool ocean breeze felt so refreshing (令人耳目一新的).
Thankfully, the beautiful coastal (沿海的) environment was not polluted (被污染的) at all.
Ultimately, nature was very generous (慷慨的) to this stunning place.

questions.question_text:
Describe a holiday destination you would like to visit. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I would love to visit the Maldives one day.
It is a coastal (沿海的) country in the Indian Ocean,
made up of many small islands.
To get there, I would take a flight from China,
and then a short boat ride to my hotel.
Honestly, the views there are truly breathtaking (美得惊人的),
with clear blue water everywhere.
To begin with, I would spend my days swimming and snorkelling near the beach,
which is a stone's throw away (近在咫尺) from most resorts.
Furthermore, the ocean air feels so refreshing (令人耳目一新的) and clean.
I think this place attracts me because the environment is very serene (宁静的),
which means I could finally relax and forget about all my stress.
In the end, the Maldives is my perfect dream holiday.

questions.question_text:
Describe a place in your country that is famous for its natural beauty. You should say:
who this person is
how you know them
what they do or have done
and explain why you find them memorable or important

chunks.text: I want to talk about Sanya,
which is a beautiful coastal city in the south of China.
To begin with, it is located on Hainan Island.
Actually, the beaches there are amazing —
the water is crystal clear (清澈见底 / 一清二楚) and the sand is very white and soft.
Honestly, nature has been very generous (慷慨的 (nature)) to this place,
giving it warm weather almost all year.
For example, many visitors love to soak up the sun (享受阳光) on the beach during winter.
I think it is famous because the sea is clean and not polluted (被污染的),
which makes it perfect for swimming and water sports.
Furthermore, the local people are very neighborly (友好的 (people)) and always help tourists feel welcome.
In the end, I strongly think Sanya is a wonderful place for tourists
because it is beautiful, warm, and easy to enjoy.

questions.question_text: Why do so many people like to live near the water?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, coastal areas offer beautiful views,
and they provide a very relaxing lifestyle.
Furthermore, living near the ocean gives people easy access
to fresh air and fun water sports.
For example, many individuals move to maritime (海事的 / 沿海的) cities
specifically to enjoy sailing or daily beach walks.

questions.question_text: What are the dangers of water pollution to society?

chunks.text: Undeniably, contaminated rivers threaten human health,
and they severely damage the local biodiversity (生物多样性).
Consequently, toxic chemicals from rapid urbanization (城市化) can easily poison drinking water supplies.
As a result, poor water security (安全) leads to terrible disease outbreaks in many developing nations.

questions.question_text: Should the government limit tourism in environmentally sensitive areas?

chunks.text: Absolutely, strict limits are necessary,
because crowds can easily destroy fragile natural habitats.
Fundamentally, prioritizing environmental conservation (保护)
is much more important than generating short-term tourism profits.
Therefore, capping visitor numbers helps turn the tide (扭转局势)
against severe ecological degradation.

questions.question_text: How important is water transportation (shipping) to the global economy?

chunks.text: In my opinion, shipping is absolutely vital,
since it enables massive international trade.
Actually, moving heavy cargo via ocean transportation (运输)
is significantly cheaper than using airplanes.
For instance, giant cargo ships continuously transport millions of tons
of electronics and clothing across the globe daily.

questions.question_text: Do you think future wars will be fought over water resources?

chunks.text: Sadly, this is highly likely,
as clean water is becoming incredibly scarce worldwide.
Essentially, climate change and overpopulation are rapidly depleting
every vital freshwater resource (资源).
Consequently, neighboring countries sharing a single drying river
might easily go to war to survive.

questions.question_text: How can individuals reduce their water consumption in daily life?

chunks.text: Realistically, small daily habits matter,
even if they seem like a drop in the ocean (沧海一粟).
Moreover, fixing leaky pipes and taking much shorter showers
can collectively save thousands of gallons.
By doing this, individual citizens can truly make waves (引起轰动 / 产生重大影响)
in global water preservation efforts.

questions.question_text:
Describe a uniform you have worn (e.g., at school or work). You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe my high school uniform,
which was quite formal (正式的), and which I wore every day.
Honestly, the dark blue color looked very serene (宁静的) and professional.
Actually, the blazer was tailored to fit like a glove (极度合身) on me.
Furthermore, wearing it made me feel quite stylish (时髦的) and incredibly elegant (优雅的).
Conversely, some students thought the design was totally outdated (过时的).
However, I liked it because the cotton fabric was highly comfortable (舒适的) and refreshing (清爽的) in summer.
Ultimately, I always looked dressed to kill (盛装打扮) for school.
Overall, I felt like a true trendsetter (引领潮流的人) in my class.

questions.question_text:
Describe a piece of clothing you like to wear on special occasions. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: Actually, the piece of clothing I like most
is a dark blue dress
that I bought at a shopping mall near my home last year.
To be honest, it was not very cheap,
but I thought it was worth the money.
To begin with, the dress looks very stylish (时髦的)
because it has a clean, modern design.
I usually wear it to formal (正式的) events
like family dinners or friends' weddings.
Honestly, I feel so elegant (优雅的) when I put it on,
which makes me walk with more confidence.
For example, at my cousin's wedding last summer,
everyone said I looked dressed to kill (盛装打扮 / 迷死人).
Furthermore, the material is very soft,
so it is also quite comfortable (舒适的) to wear for long hours.
In the end, it is my favourite dress
because it makes me feel both beautiful and happy.

questions.question_text:
Describe a traditional piece of clothing from your country. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I want to talk about the Qipao,
which is a traditional Chinese dress.
To begin with, it is usually worn by women,
and it looks very elegant.
Actually, the Qipao has a long history in China.
It has a high collar and a tight shape,
so it can fit like a glove (极其合身) on the body.
Honestly, some people think it looks outdated (过时的),
but I strongly disagree.
For example, many young women wear it at weddings and important festivals
because it looks so refreshing (清爽的 (look)) and beautiful.
In fact, I think the Qipao is a real trendsetter (引领潮流的人) in modern Chinese fashion,
because it connects old culture with new style,
which makes young people feel proud of their history.
The soft, serene (宁静的 (color)) colors of the Qipao also make it very special.

questions.question_text: Why do some companies and schools require people to wear uniforms?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, uniforms enforce strict uniformity (一致性),
which instantly creates a sense of equality.
Specifically, removing brand-name clothing prevents poorer students
from becoming targets for cruel bullying.
As a result, a shared identity (身份) helps everyone focus on their actual work
rather than their fashion choices.

questions.question_text: Do you think people are judged too much by the clothes they wear?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, society heavily judges appearances,
because clothes often signal a person's financial status (地位).
Basically, human beings naturally form quick first impressions
based entirely on visual cues.
Therefore, people tend to judge a book by its cover (以貌取人)
when they meet someone dressed in cheap clothing.

questions.question_text: How has fashion changed in your country in recent years?

chunks.text: Undeniably, fashion has become much more diverse,
and western styles are extremely popular.
Furthermore, young people use clothing as a powerful form
of bold self expression (表达) today.
For instance, teenagers now mix traditional garments with modern sneakers
to create totally unique outfits.

questions.question_text: Is it necessary to follow the latest fashion trends?

chunks.text: In my opinion, chasing trends is completely unnecessary,
as personal comfort matters more.
Actually, being a slave to fashion (时尚奴隶) is exhausting
and extremely expensive over time.
Consequently, a fashion victim (盲目追求时尚的人) will waste thousands of dollars
buying bizarre clothes they only wear once.

questions.question_text: Do you think expensive designer clothes are worth the money?

chunks.text: Honestly, luxury brands are rarely worth the huge price tag,
though their quality is good.
Essentially, you are mostly paying for the famous logo
rather than the actual textile (纺织品) itself.
Therefore, you can easily find durable, stylish clothes at normal stores
for a fraction of the cost.

questions.question_text: Does the "fast fashion" industry (cheap, disposable clothing) harm the environment?

chunks.text: Absolutely, fast fashion is disastrous,
because it pollutes rivers and creates massive waste.
Fundamentally, throwing away cheap shirts constantly drains every natural resource (资源) we have left.
Instead, promoting clothing recycling and mindful conservation (节约) is the only way to save our planet.

questions.question_text:
Describe an important traditional festival in your country. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: To begin with, I want to describe the Spring Festival,
which is highly traditional (传统的),
and which brings families together.
Honestly, the atmosphere is incredibly festive (节日的) and vibrant (充满活力的) everywhere.
Actually, eating dumplings together is a deeply meaningful (有意义的) and ceremonial (仪式的) custom.
Furthermore, we always dress in formal (正式的) and elegant (优雅的) red clothing to welcome the new year.
Later, my friends and I usually go out to paint the town red (狂欢) with fireworks.
Consequently, we always manage to have a ball (玩得开心).
Ultimately, since the more the merrier (人越多越热闹), large gatherings are simply the best part.

questions.question_text:
Describe a party or celebration you enjoyed attending. You should say:
when this happened
where it took place
what happened during it
and explain how you felt about this experience

chunks.text: I want to talk about my cousin's wedding party last year.
To begin with, it was a very traditional (传统的) Chinese wedding,
so there were many customs to follow.
Honestly, the whole room had a very festive (节日的 / 喜庆的) feeling,
with red decorations everywhere.
Almost all of my family members were there,
including grandparents, aunts, and uncles.
Actually, we ate a big dinner together, played fun games, and took lots of photos.
For example, we did a toast for the couple,
which made the moment very meaningful (有意义的).
The music was loud and the dancing was so vibrant (充满活力的)
that everyone got up to join in.
In the end, we really decided to paint the town red (狂欢)
and stayed until very late.
Of course, I enjoyed every single moment of it.

questions.question_text:
Describe a national holiday in your country. You should say:
where this place is
when you visited or first went there
what you did or saw there
and explain why this place is special to you

chunks.text: I'd like to talk about Chinese New Year,
which falls on the first day of the lunar calendar.
To begin with, this holiday has a very long history.
Actually, people say it started as a way to scare away a monster
called "Nian" using loud sounds and red decorations.
Honestly, my family always plans big meals and visits for this day.
We wear formal (正式的) new clothes
and take part in ceremonial (仪式的) rituals like offering food to our ancestors.
To be honest, my grandmother always cooks in a very elegant (优雅的) way,
which makes everything feel extra special.
Of course, we invite lots of relatives
because we believe the more the merrier (人越多越热闹).
In the end, we always have a ball (玩得极开心) together.
I think this holiday is significant because it brings families closer
and keeps our culture alive.

questions.question_text: Is globalization causing young people to lose interest in traditional festivals?

chunks.text: Admittedly, Western holidays are trending,
but local traditions remain deeply important to the youth.
While globalization (全球化) introduces new celebrations,
young people still respect their roots to maintain cultural identity (认同感).
For instance, teenagers might celebrate Christmas for fun,
but they still prioritize the Lunar New Year.

questions.question_text: Do you think festivals have become too commercialized (focused on money/shopping)?

chunks.text: Unfortunately, heavy marketing has ruined holidays,
because businesses only care about maximizing their profits.
Consequently, the extreme commercialization (商业化) of events like Valentine's Day
pressures people into buying expensive, useless gifts.
As a result, the original emotional expression (表达) of love
is entirely replaced by pure consumerism.

questions.question_text: Why are public holidays important for the workforce?

chunks.text: Undeniably, days off are crucial,
since they prevent severe burnout among exhausted employees.
Fundamentally, returning to work feeling rested
and in high spirits (兴高采烈) drastically improves overall productivity.
Therefore, a long weekend allows stressed office workers
to properly rest and spend time with their families.

questions.question_text: How do festivals help to preserve a country's culture?

chunks.text: Essentially, annual celebrations pass down customs,
which actively prevents historical knowledge from dying completely.
Furthermore, practicing old rituals guarantees the preservation (保存) of a nation's unique intangible heritage (遗产).
For example, teaching children how to make traditional mooncakes
helps to keep tradition alive (传承传统) forever.

questions.question_text: Should immigrants learn about the festivals of their new country?

chunks.text: Absolutely, learning local customs is vital,
as it promotes smooth social integration (融合).
Actually, participating in national events helps newcomers feel included
in the modern cultural melting pot (大熔炉).
As an illustration, an expat joining a local Thanksgiving dinner
quickly builds strong bonds with their new neighbors.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between how children and adults enjoy festivals?

chunks.text: Generally speaking, kids love the games,
whereas adults appreciate the rare chance to relax.
Basically, children focus entirely on receiving fun toys
or eating lots of sweet candy.
Conversely, tired parents enjoy festivals simply by catching up with old friends
over a very quiet meal.
